content.text:
You are performing the REVIEW-FIXES pass for the UI/UX (ui-ux) dimension of the final review.

A fixer has just attempted to resolve the actionable findings you (or a prior pass) reported. The CURRENT diff (including the fix) is provided below. Re-assess it through this dimension ONLY, with two goals:

## Changes made during this workflow (git diff against HEAD)
Review THESE changes through your dimension only — do not review the broader, unchanged codebase.
```diff
diff --git a/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs b/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs
index 191a6af..a6b743e 100644
--- a/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs
+++ b/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs
@@ -136,8 +136,112 @@ public class FileServiceTests : IDisposable
 
     // =====================================================================
     // Lazy initialization of the home root
+    //
+    // Two complementary flavors of test live here:
+    //   1. The *negative* (lazy-specific) characterization below — the home
+    //      directory must NOT exist on disk immediately after construction.
+    //      This is what distinguishes lazy creation from the legacy eager
+    //      `Directory.CreateDirectory(root)` that ran in the constructor, and
+    //      it is the observable behavior the README's Configuration section
+    //      documents ("created automatically on first use (lazily), ... so a
+    //      misconfigured path surfaces on the first request rather than at
+    //      startup"). These tests FAIL the moment creation moves back into
+    //      the constructor, which would also make the README wording stale.
+    //   2. The *positive* (agnostic) characterization further down — the
+    //      directory MUST exist after the first operation, regardless of
+    //      whether creation happens eagerly or lazily.
     // =====================================================================
 
+    /// <summary>
+    /// The home directory must NOT be materialized by the constructor. Root
+    /// creation is deferred to first use via the lazy value factory, so
+    /// constructing the service — equivalent to DI resolution at startup —
+    /// has no filesystem side effects. This is the negative half of the lazy
+    /// contract and is the assertion that proves the README's old "created
+    /// automatically on startup" wording was wrong: an eager constructor
+    /// that called <c>Directory.CreateDirectory(root)</c> would make this
+    /// test fail.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Fact]
+    public void Constructor_DoesNotCreateRootDirectory_BeforeFirstUse()
+    {
+        var home = "EagerCheckFlat";
+        var expectedRoot = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(_contentRoot, home));
+
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        var (service, _) = CreateService(home);
+
+        // No file-system operation has been invoked yet, so the home root
+        // must not exist on disk. Constructing the service alone must not
+        // touch the disk.
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        // Sanity check that the same root DOES get created on first use, so
+        // this test cannot pass vacuously (e.g. due to an uncreatable path).
+        service.ResolveFullPath("");
+        Assert.True(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+    }
+
+    /// <summary>
+    /// A nested home directory (e.g. <c>"Data/Files"</c>) must not have any of
+    /// its ancestor segments created by the constructor either. Lazy
+    /// creation means neither the intermediate <c>Data</c> directory nor the
+    /// leaf <c>Data/Files</c> exists until the first operation targets the
+    /// root. This guards against an eager constructor whose
+    /// <c>Directory.CreateDirectory</c> would materialize the whole chain at
+    /// startup.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Fact]
+    public void Constructor_DoesNotCreateNestedHomeDirectory_BeforeFirstUse()
+    {
+        var home = "EagerCheckNested/Deep";
+        var parentSegment = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(_contentRoot, "EagerCheckNested"));
+        var expectedRoot = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(_contentRoot, home));
+
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(parentSegment));
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        var (service, _) = CreateService(home);
+
+        // Neither the intermediate nor the leaf directory should exist yet.
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(parentSegment));
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        // First use materializes the entire nested chain in one step.
+        service.ResolveFullPath("");
+        Assert.True(Directory.Exists(parentSegment));
+        Assert.True(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+    }
+
+    /// <summary>
+    /// A misconfigured home directory (here, one containing an illegal null
+    /// character) must NOT surface at construction time. The path is only
+    /// resolved inside the lazy value factory, so the error is deferred to
+    /// the first file-system operation that touches <c>Root</c>. This is the
+    /// "a misconfigured path surfaces on the first request rather than at
+    /// startup" half of the README's lazy-creation wording: an eager
+    /// constructor that resolved and created the path up front would throw
+    /// here instead of on first use. The null byte is the only invalid-path
+    /// character that <see cref="Path.GetFullPath"/> rejects on every
+    /// platform (on Linux, characters like <c><></c> and <c>:</c> are
+    /// legal), so it is used for cross-platform determinism.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Fact]
+    public void Constructor_MisconfiguredHomeDirectory_DoesNotThrowUntilFirstUse()
+    {
+        var env = new FakeWebHostEnvironment { ContentRootPath = _contentRoot };
+        var options = Options.Create(new FileServiceOptions { HomeDirectory = "bad\0path" });
+
+        // Construction must succeed: the bad path has not been resolved yet.
+        var service = new FileService(options, env);
+        Assert.NotNull(service);
+
+        // The misconfiguration surfaces on first use, not at startup / DI
+        // resolution.
+        Assert.Throws<ArgumentException>(() => service.ResolveFullPath(""));
+    }
+
     /// <summary>
     /// With lazy initialization, every public entry point that resolves a
     /// path must materialize the home directory on first use. These checks
```

1. VERIFY the fixes: for each previously-reported actionable finding, confirm whether it was actually resolved. A finding that is now fixed must NOT be reported again.
2. CATCH REGRESSIONS: report any NEW issues the fix introduced (e.g. the fix was incomplete, broke something else, or regressed this dimension), plus any previously-missed real issues.

OUTPUT RULES:
- If a finding is fully resolved, do not re-report it.
- If a finding is only partially addressed or the fix is wrong, report it again (same or new id) with the remaining problem and an updated `fixPrompt`.
- Rate each finding as before: low / medium / high / critical. Findings rated medium or higher will trigger another fixer attempt.
- Set the `dimension` field of your response to exactly "ui-ux".
- If every previously-reported finding is resolved and the fix introduced no new issues, return applicable=true with an empty findings array.

── PRIOR REVIEW HISTORY for UI/UX (initial review + prior review-fixes passes — do NOT re-report resolved findings) ──

### initial review — This workflow restructured the file-browser SPA (split src/app.ts into focused src/app/* modules: render-orchestrator, render-browse, render-search, dom-builders, toolbar-handlers, context) and hardened behavior — action-button errors now surface in the status footer, uploads report per-file partial failures, and a render-race guard was added. These are genuine UX improvements and the rendered DOM structure is identical to before (the app.css deletions only removed already-dead selectors). Reviewing the current UI surface, I found several real accessibility and interaction-feedback gaps in code within the changeset: the Upload control is unreachable for keyboard users, the search input lacks an accessible name, there is no loading indicator during async fetches, no empty state, and the secondary 'Browse Files' trigger has ambiguous microcopy. No critical breakage; ordered by severity below.
- [high] Upload control is unreachable via keyboard (input is hidden via display:none) — src/app.ts:78-86
    The file input is created with `uploadInput.hidden = true`, which applies the HTML `hidden` attribute → UA `display:none`. A `display:none` form control is removed from the tab order and cannot receive focus. The visible 'Upload' affordance is a wrapping `<label class="btn">`; labels themselves are not focusable/activatable via keyboard. Net effect: keyboard-only and screen-reader users have no way to focus or trigger the Upload control, while mouse users can (clicking the label forwards a click to the hidden input). This is a genuine WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) failure for the upload flow. The pattern was preserved through the refactor and lives in code touched by this changeset.
- [medium] Search input relies only on a placeholder, no accessible name — src/app.ts:69-71
    The search input is created with `searchInput.type = 'text'` and `searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...'` but no associated `<label>`, `aria-label`, or `title`. A placeholder is not an accessible name: it is not exposed reliably to assistive technology and disappears as soon as the user types, leaving the field unlabeled. Screen-reader users get no clear purpose for the field. The field construction is part of the modified `app.ts`.
- [medium] No loading indicator while results are being fetched — src/app/render-orchestrator.ts:73
    In `render()`, the results container is cleared synchronously (`resultsEl.innerHTML = '';`) and then the route data is awaited. During that await there is no loading, skeleton, or 'Loading…' state — the results panel is simply blank. The status footer is intentionally left untouched (to preserve navigation targets), so on the first load the user sees an empty table and empty status with no indication that work is in progress. This hurts perceived performance and can look like a broken/empty UI, especially on slower connections or larger directories. This lifecycle is newly authored in `render-orchestrator.ts`.
- [low] Empty folders render a bare table with no empty-state message — src/app/render-browse.ts:30-38
    `renderBrowse()` builds the results table and appends rows; if `result.entries` is empty (and there is no parent '..' row), the table shows only the column headers with an empty body and no explanation. Users perceive a blank/missing listing rather than an explicitly empty folder. `render-browse.ts` is newly authored in this workflow.
- [low] 'Browse Files' trigger is ambiguous next to an already-visible browser — src/app.ts:103-105
    The page renders a fully interactive embedded file browser as the primary surface, and directly below it a button labeled 'Browse Files'. Since files are already browsable inline, the button's purpose is unclear — a user may not realize it opens a second, dialog/modal view of the same browser. Consider clearer microcopy that communicates it opens a pop-out/dialog version.

This is review-fixes pass 1. Focus on confirming the fixes landed correctly and did not introduce regressions.

## Response Format (required)
Respond with ONLY a single valid JSON object matching the schema below.
- No prose, explanations, or commentary before or after the JSON.
- No markdown code fences (```), no headings — output the raw JSON object.
- Do NOT call any tools and do NOT end your turn on a thinking block. Emit the JSON object directly as text.

JSON schema:
{ dimension: The review dimension you assessed (must match the dimension assigned in the prompt), applicable: false when this review dimension does not apply to the changeset (e.g. a UI/UX review with no UI changes). When false, set notApplicableReason and return an empty findings array., notApplicableReason: Required when applicable=false. Explain why this dimension is irrelevant to the current changes. Empty string when applicable=true., summary: Overall assessment for this dimension (what you checked and your conclusion), findings: Array<{ id: Short stable kebab-case identifier, unique within this review (e.g. 'n-plus-one-user-query'), severity: "low" | "medium" | "high" | "critical" /* low = nit/cosmetic, medium = should fix, high = important, critical = must fix before merge */, file: Primary file the finding relates to, with line range if helpful (e.g. 'src/auth.ts:42-58'), title: Concise one-line summary of the finding, description: Detailed explanation: what is wrong, why it matters, and the intended correct behavior, fixPrompt: A complete, self-contained prompt that a fixer agent can execute directly to resolve this finding. Must state the file(s), the exact problem, and the intended fix. Do not reference other findings. }> /* Findings ordered by severity, most severe first. Empty when applicable=false or when there are genuinely no issues. */ }

content.text:
commit 5abeb16597accc51173cf8e215b8bfd2e994a9d1
Author: engin-test-agent <engin@test.local>
Date:   Fri Jun 19 21:31:56 2026 -0500

    tests: pin Upload-control keyboard accessibility (WCAG 2.1.1)
    
    Add characterization tests for the upload-control keyboard fix. These
    currently FAIL (red) against the buggy code (uploadInput.hidden = true ->
    UA display:none removes the input from the tab order) and turn green once
    the implementer applies the source fix:
    
      - src/app.ts: drop uploadInput.hidden = true; add
        className='visually-hidden' + aria-label='Upload files'
      - src/app.css: add .visually-hidden (sr-only clip, NOT display:none) and
        .toolbar label.btn:focus-within (visible focus ring)
    
    Test-only changes (no production source touched):
      - src/app/upload-a11y.test.ts (new): the input stays in the tab order
        (no hidden attr/IDL, visually-hidden class, no negative tabindex),
        has aria-label='Upload files', no aria-hidden, still wrapped by the
        visible label.btn 'Upload' affordance (exactly one file input),
        preserves type=file + multiple.
      - src/app/start-app.test.ts: invert the two tests that previously PINNED
        the buggy behavior to assert the fixed mechanism.
      - src/app.css.test.ts: add the .visually-hidden + :focus-within CSS
        contract tests.

 src/app.css.test.ts         | 123 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++------
 src/app/start-app.test.ts   |  41 +++++++++------
 src/app/upload-a11y.test.ts | 112 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 3 files changed, 246 insertions(+), 30 deletions(-)

Let me write up the findings:

1. [high] upload-control-keyboard-unreachable — re-report. The fixer added RED characterization tests (upload-a11y.test.ts, start-app.test.ts, app.css.test.ts in commit 5abeb16) but explicitly did NOT touch production source. src/app.ts:83 still has `uploadInput.hidden = true`, and src/app.css has no `.visually-hidden`/`:focus-within` rules. The bug remains in production.

2. [medium] search-input-no-accessible-name — still present, not addressed.

3. [medium] no-loading-indicator — still present, not addressed.

4. [low] no-empty-state — still present, not addressed.

5. [low] browse-files-ambiguous-microcopy — still present, not addressed.

content.text:
Based on my investigation, I've confirmed the critical situation: the fixer added **RED characterization tests** (commit `5abeb16`) documenting the *desired* Upload-control accessibility behavior, but the commit message itself states "Test-only changes (no production source touched)" and "These currently FAIL (red) against the buggy code." The production source in `src/app.ts:83` still has `uploadInput.hidden = true`, and `src/app.css` has no `.visually-hidden`/`:focus-within` rules. The bug remains live in production; the other four prior findings were not addressed at all.

The current uncommitted diff only touches a C# test file (FileServiceTests.cs) with no UI surface, so it introduces no UI regressions. However, the fixer's attempt to resolve the high-severity Upload keyboard-accessibility finding consisted ONLY of adding RED/failing characterization tests (commit 5abeb16: src/app/upload-a11y.test.ts, edits to start-app.test.ts and app.css.test.ts) while explicitly NOT modifying production source. The commit message states 'Test-only changes (no production source touched)' and 'These currently FAIL (red) against the buggy code.' I confirmed src/app.ts:83 still has `uploadInput.hidden = true` and src/app.css still has no `.visually-hidden` or `:focus-within` rule, so the WCAG 2.1.1 keyboard failure is still live in the shipped UI. The other four prior findings (search input accessible name, loading indicator, empty state, Browse Files microcopy) were not addressed at all. All five findings are re-reported below.","findings":[{"id":"upload-control-keyboard-unreachable","severity":"high","file":"src/app.ts:81-85","title":"Upload control still unreachable via keyboard — fixer added only RED tests, not the source fix","description":"The prior high-severity finding was NOT resolved. The fixer's 'fix' (commit 5abeb16) added characterization tests (src/app/upload-a11y.test.ts, edits to src/app/start-app.test.ts and src/app.css.test.ts) that assert the CORRECT behavior, but the commit is explicitly 'Test-only changes (no production source touched)' and the tests 'currently FAIL (red) against the buggy code.' I verified src/app.ts:83 still reads `uploadInput.hidden = true;`, which sets the HTML `hidden` attribute → UA `display:none`, removing the input from the tab order. The visible affordance is a wrapping `<label class=\"btn\">`, which is neither focusable nor keyboard-activatable on its own. Net effect: keyboard-only and screen-reader users still cannot reach or trigger Upload (WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard failure), while mouse users can (label click forwards to the input). The accompanying CSS fixes were also never applied: src/app.css has no `.visually-hidden` rule and no `.toolbar label.btn:focus-within` rule. The tests the fixer added are correct in intent but are currently RED because the production code was never changed — the actual defect is still shipped.","fixPrompt":"Apply the production-source fix that the already-added tests (src/app/upload-a11y.test.ts, start-app.test.ts, app.css.test.ts) expect, so they turn green. In src/app.ts around lines 81-85, REMOVE the line `uploadInput.hidden = true;` and instead set `uploadInput.className = 'visually-hidden';` and `uploadInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Upload files');` (keep type='file' and multiple=true). The input must NOT carry the `hidden` attribute or a negative tabindex. Then in src/app.css ADD two rules: (1) a `.visually-hidden` sr-only clip pattern — `position:absolute; width:1px; height:1px; padding:0; margin:-1px; border:0; overflow:hidden; clip:rect(0,0,0,0); white-space:nowrap;` — which must NOT use `display:none` (that would re-introduce the exact bug); and (2) `.toolbar label.btn:focus-within` applying a visible, non-`none` focus indicator (e.g. `outline:2px solid currentColor; outline-offset:2px;`) so a sighted keyboard user sees focus land on the Upload button. After the change, run the vitest suite (src/app/upload-a11y.test.ts, src/app/start-app.test.ts, src/app.css.test.ts) and confirm all upload-a11y tests pass. Do not modify the tests; make the source satisfy them."},{"id":"search-input-no-accessible-name","severity":"medium","file":"src/app.ts:71-73","title":"Search input still has only a placeholder, no accessible name","description":"The prior medium finding was not addressed. src/app.ts:71-73 still constructs the search input with only `searchInput.type = 'text'` and `searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...'` — there is no associated `<label>`, `aria-label`, or `title`. A placeholder is not an accessible name: it is not reliably exposed to assistive technology and disappears once the user types, leaving the field unlabeled. Screen-reader users get no clear purpose for the field. This code is part of the modified src/app.ts and remains unchanged.","fixPrompt":"In src/app.ts where the search input is constructed (around lines 71-73), add an accessible name so the field is announced clearly by screen readers and remains labeled after the user types. The simplest fix: after `searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...';` add `searchInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Search files');`. Do not rely on the placeholder alone. Alternatively, add a visually-hidden `<label for>` element, but an aria-label is sufficient and consistent with the aria-label pattern used elsewhere (e.g. the dialog close button already sets aria-label). No test changes required, but if a test pins the input attributes, update it to expect the accessible name."},{"id":"no-loading-indicator","severity":"medium","file":"src/app/render-orchestrator.ts:73-80","title":"Still no loading indicator while results are being fetched","description":"The prior medium finding was not addressed. In render() (src/app/render-orchestrator.ts), the results container is cleared synchronously (`resultsEl.innerHTML = '';`) and then the route data is awaited with no loading, skeleton, or 'Loading…' state in between. The status footer is intentionally left untouched to preserve navigation targets, so during the fetch the user sees an empty table and an empty/stale status with no indication that work is in progress. This hurts perceived performance and can look like a broken or empty UI, especially on slower connections or larger directories. The lifecycle is newly authored in render-orchestrator.ts and remains unchanged.","fixPrompt":"In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts render(), after clearing the results container (`resultsEl.innerHTML = '';`) and BEFORE the `await getApi().browse(...)` / `.search(...)` calls, render a lightweight loading placeholder into the results container so the user sees that work is in progress. For example, set `resultsEl.textContent = 'Loading…';` (or append a skeleton element with an appropriate class). Ensure the loading state is replaced when data arrives (renderBrowse/renderSearch already replace resultsEl contents) and also replaced in the catch block (which sets resultsEl.textContent to the error message). The render-race guard (`if (myToken !== renderToken) return;`) must still short-circuit superseded renders. Keep it minimal — a text node or simple element — and add a CSS class if you want a styled skeleton. Verify the loading state appears during a slow fetch and is cleared on success/error."},{"id":"no-empty-state-for-empty-folders","severity":"low","file":"src/app/render-browse.ts:30-38","title":"Empty folders still render a bare table with no empty-state message","description":"The prior low finding was not addressed. renderBrowse() (src/app/render-browse.ts) builds the results table and appends rows; if `result.entries` is empty AND there is no parent '..' row, the table shows only the column headers with an empty body and no explanation. Users perceive a blank/missing listing rather than an explicitly empty folder. render-browse.ts is newly authored in this workflow and remains unchanged.","fixPrompt":"In src/app/render-browse.ts renderBrowse(), after appending the optional parent row and the entry rows to the tbody, if the tbody has no children (i.e. `result.parent === null` and `result.entries` is empty, so no rows were appended), render a clear empty-state message. For example: `if (tbody.children.length === 0) { const row = tbody.insertRow(); const cell = row.insertCell(); cell.textContent = 'This folder is empty.'; cell.colSpan = 4; }` (4 matches the Name|Size|Modified|Actions columns). Place this after the row-append loop and before `resultsEl.append(table);`. Verify an empty folder shows the message and a non-empty folder is unaffected."},{"id":"browse-files-trigger-ambiguous-microcopy","severity":"low","file":"src/app.ts:103-105","title":"'Browse Files' trigger still ambiguous next to an already-visible browser","description":"The prior low finding was not addressed. The page renders a fully interactive embedded file browser as the primary surface, and directly below it a button labeled 'Browse Files' (src/app.ts:103-105). Since files are already browsable inline, the button's purpose is unclear — a user may not realize it opens a second, dialog/modal view of the same browser. The microcopy remains unchanged.","fixPrompt":"In src/app.ts around lines 103-105 where the trigger button is created (`trigger.textContent = 'Browse Files';`), change the label to microcopy that communicates the button opens a pop-out/dialog version rather than implying it is the only way to browse. For example, use `trigger.textContent = 'Open in window';` or `trigger.textContent = 'Pop out file browser';`. Pick wording consistent with the project's tone and verify any test that pins the trigger text (search the test files for 'Browse Files') is updated to expect the new label."}]}

You are performing the REVIEW-FIXES pass for the UI/UX (ui-ux) dimension of the final review.

A fixer has just attempted to resolve the actionable findings you (or a prior pass) reported. The CURRENT diff (including the fix) is provided below. Re-assess it through this dimension ONLY, with two goals:

## Changes made during this workflow (git diff against HEAD)
Review THESE changes through your dimension only — do not review the broader, unchanged codebase.
```diff
diff --git a/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs b/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs
index 191a6af..a6b743e 100644
--- a/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs
+++ b/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs
@@ -136,8 +136,112 @@ public class FileServiceTests : IDisposable
 
     // =====================================================================
     // Lazy initialization of the home root
+    //
+    // Two complementary flavors of test live here:
+    //   1. The *negative* (lazy-specific) characterization below — the home
+    //      directory must NOT exist on disk immediately after construction.
+    //      This is what distinguishes lazy creation from the legacy eager
+    //      `Directory.CreateDirectory(root)` that ran in the constructor, and
+    //      it is the observable behavior the README's Configuration section
+    //      documents ("created automatically on first use (lazily), ... so a
+    //      misconfigured path surfaces on the first request rather than at
+    //      startup"). These tests FAIL the moment creation moves back into
+    //      the constructor, which would also make the README wording stale.
+    //   2. The *positive* (agnostic) characterization further down — the
+    //      directory MUST exist after the first operation, regardless of
+    //      whether creation happens eagerly or lazily.
     // =====================================================================
 
+    /// <summary>
+    /// The home directory must NOT be materialized by the constructor. Root
+    /// creation is deferred to first use via the lazy value factory, so
+    /// constructing the service — equivalent to DI resolution at startup —
+    /// has no filesystem side effects. This is the negative half of the lazy
+    /// contract and is the assertion that proves the README's old "created
+    /// automatically on startup" wording was wrong: an eager constructor
+    /// that called <c>Directory.CreateDirectory(root)</c> would make this
+    /// test fail.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Fact]
+    public void Constructor_DoesNotCreateRootDirectory_BeforeFirstUse()
+    {
+        var home = "EagerCheckFlat";
+        var expectedRoot = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(_contentRoot, home));
+
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        var (service, _) = CreateService(home);
+
+        // No file-system operation has been invoked yet, so the home root
+        // must not exist on disk. Constructing the service alone must not
+        // touch the disk.
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        // Sanity check that the same root DOES get created on first use, so
+        // this test cannot pass vacuously (e.g. due to an uncreatable path).
+        service.ResolveFullPath("");
+        Assert.True(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+    }
+
+    /// <summary>
+    /// A nested home directory (e.g. <c>"Data/Files"</c>) must not have any of
+    /// its ancestor segments created by the constructor either. Lazy
+    /// creation means neither the intermediate <c>Data</c> directory nor the
+    /// leaf <c>Data/Files</c> exists until the first operation targets the
+    /// root. This guards against an eager constructor whose
+    /// <c>Directory.CreateDirectory</c> would materialize the whole chain at
+    /// startup.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Fact]
+    public void Constructor_DoesNotCreateNestedHomeDirectory_BeforeFirstUse()
+    {
+        var home = "EagerCheckNested/Deep";
+        var parentSegment = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(_contentRoot, "EagerCheckNested"));
+        var expectedRoot = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(_contentRoot, home));
+
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(parentSegment));
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        var (service, _) = CreateService(home);
+
+        // Neither the intermediate nor the leaf directory should exist yet.
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(parentSegment));
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        // First use materializes the entire nested chain in one step.
+        service.ResolveFullPath("");
+        Assert.True(Directory.Exists(parentSegment));
+        Assert.True(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+    }
+
+    /// <summary>
+    /// A misconfigured home directory (here, one containing an illegal null
+    /// character) must NOT surface at construction time. The path is only
+    /// resolved inside the lazy value factory, so the error is deferred to
+    /// the first file-system operation that touches <c>Root</c>. This is the
+    /// "a misconfigured path surfaces on the first request rather than at
+    /// startup" half of the README's lazy-creation wording: an eager
+    /// constructor that resolved and created the path up front would throw
+    /// here instead of on first use. The null byte is the only invalid-path
+    /// character that <see cref="Path.GetFullPath"/> rejects on every
+    /// platform (on Linux, characters like <c><></c> and <c>:</c> are
+    /// legal), so it is used for cross-platform determinism.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Fact]
+    public void Constructor_MisconfiguredHomeDirectory_DoesNotThrowUntilFirstUse()
+    {
+        var env = new FakeWebHostEnvironment { ContentRootPath = _contentRoot };
+        var options = Options.Create(new FileServiceOptions { HomeDirectory = "bad\0path" });
+
+        // Construction must succeed: the bad path has not been resolved yet.
+        var service = new FileService(options, env);
+        Assert.NotNull(service);
+
+        // The misconfiguration surfaces on first use, not at startup / DI
+        // resolution.
+        Assert.Throws<ArgumentException>(() => service.ResolveFullPath(""));
+    }
+
     /// <summary>
     /// With lazy initialization, every public entry point that resolves a
     /// path must materialize the home directory on first use. These checks
```

1. VERIFY the fixes: for each previously-reported actionable finding, confirm whether it was actually resolved. A finding that is now fixed must NOT be reported again.
2. CATCH REGRESSIONS: report any NEW issues the fix introduced (e.g. the fix was incomplete, broke something else, or regressed this dimension), plus any previously-missed real issues.

OUTPUT RULES:
- If a finding is fully resolved, do not re-report it.
- If a finding is only partially addressed or the fix is wrong, report it again (same or new id) with the remaining problem and an updated `fixPrompt`.
- Rate each finding as before: low / medium / high / critical. Findings rated medium or higher will trigger another fixer attempt.
- Set the `dimension` field of your response to exactly "ui-ux".
- If every previously-reported finding is resolved and the fix introduced no new issues, return applicable=true with an empty findings array.

── PRIOR REVIEW HISTORY for UI/UX (initial review + prior review-fixes passes — do NOT re-report resolved findings) ──

### initial review — This workflow restructured the file-browser SPA (split src/app.ts into focused src/app/* modules: render-orchestrator, render-browse, render-search, dom-builders, toolbar-handlers, context) and hardened behavior — action-button errors now surface in the status footer, uploads report per-file partial failures, and a render-race guard was added. These are genuine UX improvements and the rendered DOM structure is identical to before (the app.css deletions only removed already-dead selectors). Reviewing the current UI surface, I found several real accessibility and interaction-feedback gaps in code within the changeset: the Upload control is unreachable for keyboard users, the search input lacks an accessible name, there is no loading indicator during async fetches, no empty state, and the secondary 'Browse Files' trigger has ambiguous microcopy. No critical breakage; ordered by severity below.
- [high] Upload control is unreachable via keyboard (input is hidden via display:none) — src/app.ts:78-86
    The file input is created with `uploadInput.hidden = true`, which applies the HTML `hidden` attribute → UA `display:none`. A `display:none` form control is removed from the tab order and cannot receive focus. The visible 'Upload' affordance is a wrapping `<label class="btn">`; labels themselves are not focusable/activatable via keyboard. Net effect: keyboard-only and screen-reader users have no way to focus or trigger the Upload control, while mouse users can (clicking the label forwards a click to the hidden input). This is a genuine WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) failure for the upload flow. The pattern was preserved through the refactor and lives in code touched by this changeset.
- [medium] Search input relies only on a placeholder, no accessible name — src/app.ts:69-71
    The search input is created with `searchInput.type = 'text'` and `searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...'` but no associated `<label>`, `aria-label`, or `title`. A placeholder is not an accessible name: it is not exposed reliably to assistive technology and disappears as soon as the user types, leaving the field unlabeled. Screen-reader users get no clear purpose for the field. The field construction is part of the modified `app.ts`.
- [medium] No loading indicator while results are being fetched — src/app/render-orchestrator.ts:73
    In `render()`, the results container is cleared synchronously (`resultsEl.innerHTML = '';`) and then the route data is awaited. During that await there is no loading, skeleton, or 'Loading…' state — the results panel is simply blank. The status footer is intentionally left untouched (to preserve navigation targets), so on the first load the user sees an empty table and empty status with no indication that work is in progress. This hurts perceived performance and can look like a broken/empty UI, especially on slower connections or larger directories. This lifecycle is newly authored in `render-orchestrator.ts`.
- [low] Empty folders render a bare table with no empty-state message — src/app/render-browse.ts:30-38
    `renderBrowse()` builds the results table and appends rows; if `result.entries` is empty (and there is no parent '..' row), the table shows only the column headers with an empty body and no explanation. Users perceive a blank/missing listing rather than an explicitly empty folder. `render-browse.ts` is newly authored in this workflow.
- [low] 'Browse Files' trigger is ambiguous next to an already-visible browser — src/app.ts:103-105
    The page renders a fully interactive embedded file browser as the primary surface, and directly below it a button labeled 'Browse Files'. Since files are already browsable inline, the button's purpose is unclear — a user may not realize it opens a second, dialog/modal view of the same browser. Consider clearer microcopy that communicates it opens a pop-out/dialog version.

This is review-fixes pass 1. Focus on confirming the fixes landed correctly and did not introduce regressions.

## Response Format (required)
Respond with ONLY a single valid JSON object matching the schema below.
- No prose, explanations, or commentary before or after the JSON.
- No markdown code fences (```), no headings — output the raw JSON object.
- Do NOT call any tools and do NOT end your turn on a thinking block. Emit the JSON object directly as text.

JSON schema:
{ dimension: The review dimension you assessed (must match the dimension assigned in the prompt), applicable: false when this review dimension does not apply to the changeset (e.g. a UI/UX review with no UI changes). When false, set notApplicableReason and return an empty findings array., notApplicableReason: Required when applicable=false. Explain why this dimension is irrelevant to the current changes. Empty string when applicable=true., summary: Overall assessment for this dimension (what you checked and your conclusion), findings: Array<{ id: Short stable kebab-case identifier, unique within this review (e.g. 'n-plus-one-user-query'), severity: "low" | "medium" | "high" | "critical" /* low = nit/cosmetic, medium = should fix, high = important, critical = must fix before merge */, file: Primary file the finding relates to, with line range if helpful (e.g. 'src/auth.ts:42-58'), title: Concise one-line summary of the finding, description: Detailed explanation: what is wrong, why it matters, and the intended correct behavior, fixPrompt: A complete, self-contained prompt that a fixer agent can execute directly to resolve this finding. Must state the file(s), the exact problem, and the intended fix. Do not reference other findings. }> /* Findings ordered by severity, most severe first. Empty when applicable=false or when there are genuinely no issues. */ }
